title: In the face of growing AI cyber threats, do middle powers have agency?
gist: As Anthropic's Claude Mythos and OpenAI's GPT-5.5-Cyber create a stark asymmetry in access to powerful AI cyber capabilities, middle powers face a choice between aligning individually with the US or China or coordinating with each other — and history from WannaCry/NotPetya suggests a rare, currently open window favors coordination.

insights.claim: Anthropic claims its Claude Mythos Preview model can find and exploit undiscovered 'zero-day' vulnerabilities in every major operating system and every major web browser when directed by a user to do so, which is why Anthropic considers it too unsafe to release publicly.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A single AI model with claimed universal zero-day discovery capability changes the cyber threat model for every government, bank, and company running standard OS/browser stacks.
insights.quote: Mythos can find and exploit undiscovered flaws, known as ‘zero-day’ vulnerabilities, in ‘every major operating system and every major web browser when directed by a user to do so’.

insights.claim: Google reported that a criminal group used AI to find a flaw in its software, showing that AI-enabled vulnerability discovery is already being weaponized by criminal actors, not just researched by labs.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Confirms the offensive-use risk is not hypothetical; more AI-assisted cyber-criminal exploits should be expected.
insights.locator: These cyber-AI models could strengthen cyber defence

insights.claim: OpenAI released its own limited cyber model, GPT-5.5-Cyber, shortly after Anthropic's Mythos, indicating the two leading US labs are now racing in parallel to deploy offensive/defensive-capable AI cyber tools.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A two-lab race in cyber-AI capability accelerates both defensive potential and the access-gap problem for everyone outside the small circle of partners.

themes.theme: Unequal access creates a global security gap

insights.claim: Anthropic's Project Glasswing grants Mythos access to only 12 named partners (mainly US tech companies and banks) plus roughly 40 other unnamed organizations, alongside the US government and its agencies.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Access is concentrated in a narrow US-centric circle, meaning most of the world's critical infrastructure operators cannot yet use the same defensive tool.

insights.claim: The UK's AI Security Institute received access to Mythos and published a public evaluation in April 2026 of its performance in simulated cyberattacks, making the UK one of the few governments with direct visibility into the model's capabilities.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shows a tiered system where a handful of allied governments get early technical insight that most others lack.

insights.claim: Anthropic reportedly briefed the Financial Stability Board on Mythos in mid-May 2026, and the EU has said Anthropic offered Mythos access after talks, while OpenAI granted access to the European Commission and 'trusted access' to verified cybersecurity defenders — but most of the world remains excluded from either lab's capabilities.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Even sympathetic major-economy regulators are getting access on a case-by-case, negotiated basis rather than systematically, foreshadowing a durable have/have-not divide.

insights.claim: The uneven access pattern risks making most governments, central banks, and organizations worldwide structurally dependent on a small number of US AI firms for their cybersecurity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Cybersecurity dependence on a few private US firms is a strategic vulnerability investors and policymakers should track as a new form of technology-supply-chain risk.

insights.claim: Keeping most of the world's regulators locked out of understanding AI's new offensive/defensive cyber capabilities does not reduce the underlying uncertainty about the threat — it just leaves those regulators unprepared for the next crisis.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Challenges the implicit safety logic of restricting model access — argues restriction itself is a source of systemic risk, not just a mitigant.

insights.claim: In 2017, a hacking group's malicious code ('worm') disrupted hospitals across England and accidentally wreaked havoc on over 200,000 computer systems globally until a young researcher found a kill switch.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Illustrates how quickly a small exploit can cascade into a global crisis through interdependent, poorly updated networks — the same dynamic AI could accelerate.

insights.claim: Weeks after the 2017 hospital-disrupting worm, another Russia-backed cybercriminal attack was described as 'the most devastating cyberattack in history,' throwing companies and governments into meltdown.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A second global-scale incident within weeks shows how fast successive cyber shocks can compound before institutions adapt.

insights.claim: Past global cyber crises teach that technical authority, trusted information, and backchannels — both between countries and between states and companies — are essential for navigating the next crisis.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete checklist for what middle powers should be building now, before the next AI-driven cyber crisis hits.

insights.claim: A well-navigated cyber crisis can generate lasting governance improvements, including new institutions, better public-private information-sharing, and cross-border rapid response capacity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frames the current AI-cyber anxiety as a potential catalyst for durable institution-building rather than pure downside risk.

themes.theme: Middle powers' strategic choice: alignment vs. coordination

insights.claim: Middle powers locked out of frontier AI cyber capability face two paths: aligning individually with either the US or China, or coordinating with each other; the author argues only the coordination path leads to real agency.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Gives a clear decision framework for middle-power governments weighing how to respond to being excluded from frontier AI cyber-defense tools.
insights.quote: Two paths are apparent: alignment or coordination. Both are born from constraint, but only one leads to agency.

insights.claim: Individual countries could pursue alignment by offering incentives such as public data, energy resources, or preferential treatment to a US or Chinese AI company in exchange for access to cutting-edge cyber capabilities or AI infrastructure support.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Identifies the concrete bargaining chips smaller states are already using or could use to court frontier AI labs.

insights.claim: OpenAI's global initiative already offers such alignment incentives in practice, for example providing nationwide chatbot access to the United Arab Emirates.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete precedent for how alignment-based deals with frontier AI labs are already playing out for partner countries.

insights.claim: Alignment with a superpower does not guarantee access to cutting-edge AI models during an actual crisis, and the link between building out AI infrastructure and improved cyber defense is weak — it will take years for cyber defense to catch up with evolving AI threats.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Undercuts the common assumption that hosting AI infrastructure or partnering with a superpower automatically buys cybersecurity — a key reason the author favors coordination over alignment.

insights.claim: The global reaction to Mythos has opened a rare window for building crisis-ready international AI-cyber policy, combining policymaker awareness, a visible threat, companies open to collaborating, trusted technical authorities, and public outcry.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Signals to policymakers that the political conditions for coordinated AI-cyber governance are unusually favorable right now and may not persist.
insights.quote: It is too good an opportunity for middle powers to miss.

insights.claim: Middle powers already have a loose institutional network for sharing information about AI risks, including the EU, UK, Kenya, and Japan among others, but it is not currently designed for political action.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This existing but underused network is the concrete institutional starting point the author says should be strengthened into a crisis-response and rapid-response mechanism.

insights.claim: Although the US is nominally part of this middle-power information-sharing network and policymakers there worry about cyber-AI models, tech companies reportedly lobbied against an earlier, stronger version of Trump's AI-cyber executive order that would have allowed a longer pre-release safety testing period.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shows industry lobbying is already shaping how much pre-release safety scrutiny frontier cyber-AI models get, a live regulatory-risk factor to watch.
